subject: UX & Research
title: Skeuomorphism and Familiarity
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on Skeuomorphism and Familiarity.

hook.text: Early iPhone apps looked like real objects: a notepad with torn pages, a bookshelf with wood grain.

buildup.text: Skeuomorphism leverages existing mental models. A trash can icon means 'delete' because real trash cans exist.

discovery.text: It helped millions adopt smartphones. Familiar metaphors made touchscreens feel safe and understandable.

twist.text: But metaphors can trap you. A calendar app that looks like paper can't show features paper never had.

climax.text: Apple shifted to flat design in iOS 7. Users had learned enough — the training wheels could come off.

punchline.text: Borrow from the real world until users don't need it.

quiz.question: Why did Apple use skeuomorphic design in early iPhones?
options: To leverage familiar real-world objects and ease the transition to touchscreens
Because flat design hadn't been invented yet
